title: The First Ten Days of Phase II in the U.S.–Israel–Iran Conflict
subtitle: From War Termination to Systemic Realignment
title_normalized: the first ten days of phase ii in the u.s.–israel–iran conflict

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan

alternate_identifiers.identifier: The First Ten Days of Phase II in the U.S.–Israel–Iran Conflict From War Termination to Systemic Realignment.pdf
The First Ten Days of Phase II in the U.S.–Israel–Iran Conflict
alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title

description: This policy brief examines the first ten analytical days of Phase II in the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict. It argues that the conflict is shifting from escalation accumulation toward systemic realignment, exposing the costs of U.S. prioritization of Israel, widening U.S.–Israel desynchronization, Iran’s more calibrated Phase II behavior, the strategic role of the Caspian–Persian Gulf linkage, and the emergence of broader resource-chain transmission beyond oil markets.
abstract: This policy brief examines the first ten analytical days of Phase II in the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict. It argues that the conflict is shifting from escalation accumulation toward systemic realignment. Phase I forced the United States to prioritize Israel in practical crisis-management terms, while Phase II exposes the costs of that prioritization across Ukraine support, European confidence, air-defense allocation, and Indo-Pacific deterrence signaling. The brief also analyzes growing U.S.–Israel desynchronization, the Netanyahu constraint, Iran’s more calibrated Phase II behavior, and the strategic role of the Caspian–Persian Gulf linkage. It further argues that the conflict is no longer only an oil-market shock, but a wider resource-chain transmission problem involving sulfur, fertilizer, LNG, petrochemicals, metals, battery inputs, and food-system exposure. The brief concludes that Phase II should be understood not simply as a path from war to peace, but as a test of whether escalation’s consequences can still be organized across regional, Eurasian, and global systems.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, information conflict, systemic risk, logistics resilience, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.source_basis: Open-source policy analysis and scenario-based assessment of the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict, alliance synchronization, corridor redundancy, energy and resource-chain exposure, and climate-linked food-security risk as of June 12, 2026.
methodology.case_context: The first ten analytical days of Phase II in the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict, following Phase I escalation accumulation and the transition toward war termination and systemic realignment.
methodology.core_problem: The central analytical problem is whether the consequences of prior escalation can be organized across regional, Eurasian, and global systems after kinetic tempo begins to decline but unresolved pressure remains active.
central_claims: Phase I forced a U.S. strategic choice and Phase II exposes the costs of that prioritization.
U.S.–Israel alignment remains durable but less synchronized as Washington prioritizes controlled recompression and Israel prioritizes deterrence restoration.
The Netanyahu constraint may complicate U.S. war-termination objectives amid Israeli domestic political uncertainty.
Iran appears more calibrated in Phase II, using diplomacy, selective retaliation, maritime ambiguity, external-node activation, and legal framing to preserve leverage.
The Caspian–Persian Gulf linkage has become central because the Persian Gulf remains the front-end coercive theater while the Caspian functions as a back-end redundancy layer.
The conflict is no longer mainly an oil shock and increasingly transmits through sulfur, fertilizer, LNG, petrochemicals, metals, battery materials, and food-system inputs.
Russia, China, and Iran appear to be moving toward a latent Eurasian community of interests rather than an open military coalition.

analytical_lenses.name: Phase Transition Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Distinguishes Phase I escalation accumulation from Phase II post-escalation management, war-termination sequencing, and systemic realignment.

analytical_lenses.name: Alliance Synchronization Analysis
analytical_lenses.description: Assesses divergence between durable security alignment and operational or political desynchronization among allies.

analytical_lenses.name: Netanyahu Constraint
analytical_lenses.description: Identifies the risk that Israeli domestic political incentives and leadership preferences may conflict with U.S. war-termination requirements.

analytical_lenses.name: Caspian–Persian Gulf Linkage
analytical_lenses.description: Frames the Persian Gulf as a front-end coercive theater and the Caspian as a back-end redundancy and pressure-absorption layer.

analytical_lenses.name: Resource-Chain Transmission
analytical_lenses.description: Extends conflict-impact analysis beyond oil prices to sulfur, fertilizer, LNG, petrochemicals, metals refining, battery materials, and food-system exposure.

analytical_lenses.name: Climate-Risk Multiplier
analytical_lenses.description: Evaluates how El Niño conditions could amplify conflict-driven resource disruption into agricultural and political stress.

analytical_lenses.name: Latent Eurasian Community of Interests
analytical_lenses.description: Interprets Russia, China, and Iran as a geographically enabled alignment that seeks insulation from isolation and dilution of U.S. coercive leverage without forming an open military coalition.

dimensions.name: U.S. Prioritization Cost
dimensions.observed_pattern: Phase II exposes reduced bandwidth for Ukraine support, European reassurance, air-defense allocation, and Indo-Pacific deterrence signaling.
dimensions.interpretation: U.S. global power remains substantial, but the margin for simultaneous crisis management is not unlimited.

dimensions.name: U.S.–Israel Desynchronization
dimensions.observed_pattern: Washington seeks controlled recompression while Israel seeks deterrence restoration and maximal constraints on Iran.
dimensions.interpretation: Alignment remains durable, but divergence over timing, risk tolerance, and settlement quality increases war-termination friction.

dimensions.name: Netanyahu Constraint
dimensions.observed_pattern: Israeli snap-election uncertainty may narrow Netanyahu’s room for compromise and increase incentives for escalation signaling or settlement obstruction.
dimensions.interpretation: The U.S. challenge is to sustain Israeli security while preventing Israeli domestic politics from derailing a wider de-escalation framework.

dimensions.name: Iran Phase II Calibration
dimensions.observed_pattern: Iran combines mediated diplomacy, selective retaliation, maritime ambiguity, external-node activation, and legal framing.
dimensions.interpretation: Iran seeks to preserve leverage without converting every pressure point into immediate escalation.

dimensions.name: Caspian–Persian Gulf Linkage
dimensions.observed_pattern: The Persian Gulf remains the front-end coercive theater while the Caspian provides back-end redundancy and threshold delay.
dimensions.interpretation: The Caspian cannot replace Gulf-scale maritime flows but can sustain selective logistics and deepen Iran’s strategic depth.

dimensions.name: Resource-Chain Transmission
dimensions.observed_pattern: The Gulf shock extends into sulfur, sulfuric acid, ammonia, urea, phosphate fertilizer, LNG, petrochemicals, metals refining, battery materials, and food-system inputs.
dimensions.interpretation: Phase II is a global political economy problem, not only a battlefield or oil-market event.

dimensions.name: El Niño Multiplier
dimensions.observed_pattern: Possible El Niño conditions could interact with fertilizer stress, fuel-cost volatility, drought, flood, and harvest anomalies.
dimensions.interpretation: Regional maritime disruption may become a delayed food-security and political shock across exposed import-dependent states.

key_findings: Phase I forced a U.S. strategic choice; Phase II is exposing its costs across Ukraine support, air-defense allocation, European confidence, and Indo-Pacific deterrence signaling.
U.S.–Israel alignment remains durable, but less synchronized as Washington seeks controlled recompression and Israel seeks deterrence restoration.
Washington may face a Netanyahu constraint as Israeli snap-election uncertainty affects compromise incentives and escalation risk.
Iran appears more calibrated in Phase II than in Phase I, combining mediated diplomacy, selective retaliation, maritime ambiguity, external-node activation, and legal framing.
The Caspian–Persian Gulf linkage has become central to Phase II because it connects coercion in the southern maritime theater with resilience across the northern Eurasian interior.

The conflict is no longer mainly an oil shock and now reaches sulfur, sulfuric acid, ammonia, urea, phosphate fertilizer, LNG, petrochemicals, metals refining, battery materials, and food-system inputs.
El Niño conditions could amplify resource-chain disruption into delayed food-security and political stress.
Russia, China, and Iran appear to be moving toward a latent Eurasian community of interests that dilutes U.S. coercive leverage without forming an open military coalition.
Phase II should be understood as a transition from military escalation to systemic realignment rather than a simple move from war to peace.
policy_recommendations: Distinguish Israeli security from Israeli escalation preferences.
Treat war termination as system design rather than as the mere cessation of strikes.
Frame Hormuz as a governance problem, not only an access problem.
Monitor the Caspian as part of the conflict system rather than as a peripheral logistics route.
Move resource-chain monitoring beyond oil to include sulfur, sulfuric acid, ammonia, urea, phosphate fertilizer, LNG, petrochemicals, metals refining, battery materials, and food-system inputs.
Integrate climate risk into conflict assessment, especially where El Niño could amplify agricultural and political stress.
Prepare for alliance divergence rather than alliance collapse.

watchpoints.indicator: U.S.–Israel public and private divergence over ceasefire sequencing, settlement terms, or follow-on strikes
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Detects whether durable alignment is becoming operationally desynchronized.

watchpoints.indicator: Israeli domestic political movement toward early elections or coalition instability
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Assesses whether the Netanyahu constraint is increasing settlement obstruction or late-stage escalation risk.

watchpoints.indicator: Iranian use of mediated diplomacy, selective retaliation, maritime ambiguity, or legal framing
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tracks whether Iran remains calibrated or returns to more escalatory retaliation loops.

watchpoints.indicator: Caspian, rail, and overland logistics patterns connected to Iran, Russia, and Central Asian transit systems
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Assesses whether northern redundancy is increasing Iran’s threshold delay and strategic depth.

watchpoints.indicator: Sulfur, fertilizer, LNG, petrochemical, metals, battery-input, and food-price stress indicators
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Detects delayed resource-chain transmission beyond oil markets.

watchpoints.indicator: ENSO / El Niño development and its interaction with fertilizer and fuel-cost pressure
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Evaluates whether climate conditions amplify conflict disruption into food-security stress.

disclaimer: This policy brief is an institutional publication of EPINOVA, prepared by Dr. Shaoyuan Wu in his capacity as Director of the Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC. The analysis is based on publicly available information and scenario-based analytical estimates and does not represent the official position of any government. The publication is intended solely for research and policy discussion purposes and does not constitute legal, military, operational, or sanctions-compliance advice.

metadata_source.notes: PDF supplied publication number, title, subtitle, author, affiliation, date, key judgments, executive summary, policy implications, limitations, conclusion, recommended citation, and disclaimer where available. Crossref registration confirmation email was reported by the author, so DOI status is marked as registered. The PDF page-1 sidebar recommended citation appears to show DOI suffix .054, but the author-submitted Crossref record and this metadata use the registered PB-55 DOI: 10.67037/epinova.pb.2026.055. The policy brief PDF itself does not contain a formal references section; the author-supplied references are therefore preserved in this metadata record under the references field.
